source_metadata.title_raw: Eric May, Esq. - A-18-055 - May 18, 2018 - Woodland

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 1102 Q Street• Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Eric May, Esq. 
Senior Deputy County Counsel 
As Acting General Counsel, 
Valley Clean Energy Alliance 
625 Court Street, Room 201 
Woodland, CA 95695 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-055 
Dear Mr. May: 
May 18, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the Board of the Valley Clean 
Energy Alliance ("VCEA") regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act 
(the "Act"). 1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions 
of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict 
of interest or Section 1090. 
Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTION 
May three members of the VCEA Board with solar panels attached to their residential or 
commercial properties take part in decisions to set net energy metering rates for VCEA's future 
service territory? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. The three members of the VCEA Board with solar panels attached to their residential 
and commercial properties may take part in decisions to set net energy metering rates as the 
decisions fall under the "public generally" exception for utility rates under Regulation 18703( e )(1 ), 
so long as the rates are applied equally, proportionally, or by the same percentage to all properties 
with renewable energy systems within VCEA 's future service territory. 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS AS PROVIDED BY THE REQUESTOR 
File No. A-18-055 
Page No. 2 
The VCEA is a joint powers authority whose members consist of the County of Yolo, the 
City of Davis, and the City of Woodland. Its Board is comprised of two elected representatives 
from each of its members' governing bodies. VCEA is one of a growing number of Community 
Choice Aggregation ("CCA") programs in California that buy or generate electricity for their 
residents and businesses within their jurisdictions. When VCEA begins operations later this year, 
VCEA will assume electricity generation from the existing investor-owned utility company, Pacific 
Gas & Electric ("PG&E"), and PG&E would continue to provide distribution and billing services. 
While individual customers can opt out of the CCA program to remain with their existing utility, in 
most communities with a CCA program, the CCA participation rate is well above 90%. 
As part of its rate-setting responsibilities, the VCEA Board establishes net energy metering 
("NEM") rates that compensate customers for the excess electricity they generate from their 
renewable energy systems, most commonly roof-top solar panels. In VCEA's territory, PG&E 
administers an NEM program for its solar customers. PG&E currently bills NEM customers 
monthly, and users receive credits, at the retail rate, for excess generation to offset later energy 
usage. Each year, PG&E calculates a user's net usage for the past 12 months. If the customer has net 
surplus generation, PG&E compensates the customer at the wholesale energy rate. It is estimated 
that about 8% of current PG&E customers in VCEA's future service territory have renewable 
energy systems, and approximately 18% of those customers generate more energy than they use 
over the course of the year. 
CCAs can adopt their own NEM policies related to their customers' solar usage. For 
example, a CCA can compensate NEM customers at the retail market rate rather than the lower 
wholesale market rate when calculating excess generation at the end of the year. Or the CCA may 
add an additional per kWh premium to the rate paid to customers. Those with net surplus generation 
in any given month could be affected financially by these policies. 
Two of the six VCEA Board members have solar panels on their personal residences. One 
of the six VCEA Board members has solar panels for his commercial business. During its 
February 8, 2018 Board meeting, the VCEA Board considered how to structure the NEM program. 
The three VCEA Board Members with solar panels were prepared to recuse themselves out of an 
abundance of caution. As a result, one member participated in the item in order to maintain a 
quorum of the Board. 
The NEM rate-setting issue will arise again in the future, and VCEA would prefer that its 
Board members participate to the greatest extent possible in order for each VCEA member to 
equally participate in important decisions. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial 
interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public 


File No. A-18-055 
Page No. 3 
official's interests set forth in Section 87103. Specific to the provided facts, the following interests 
are relevant to your request for advice: 
• 
An interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of 
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, 
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).) If the 
VCEA Board member who uses solar panels for his business has a direct or indirect 
investment of $2,000 or more in the business or hold a position of management, the 
Board member has an interest in his business. 
• 
An interest in real property in which the officer has a direct or indirect interest of$2,000 
or more. (Section 87103(b).) The two VCEA Board members with solar panels attached 
to their residences each have a property interest if they have a direct or indirect interest 
of $2,000 or more in their respective residences. Additionally, the Board member who 
uses solar panels for his business has an interest in the property if his business owns the 
property.2 
• 
An interest in any source of income to the official which aggregates to $500 or more 
within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103( c ). ) The Board member who uses 
solar panels for his business has an interest in any income received from his business if 
the income aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the VCEA Board's 
decision. 
• 
An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate 
family. (Section 87103.) This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. An 
official always has an interest in his or her personal finances.3 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
Standards for foreseeability vary depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in a 
governmental decision. An official's financial interest is explicitly involved in a decision, and is 
presumed to be reasonably foreseeable, if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a 
decision before the official or the official's agency. (Regulation 18701(a).) Regulation 1870l(a) 
establishes that a financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the 
issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or 
contract with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental decision affecting a real 
property financial interest as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(l)-(6). (Regulation 18701(a).) For 
a financial interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision al issue, the financial effect of the 
2 Section 87103 provides: "For purposes of this section, indirect investment or interest means any investment 
or interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a 
business entity or trust in which the official, the official's agents, spouse, and dependent children own directly, 
indirectly, or beneficially a I 0-percent interest or greater." Therefore, if the Board member owns a l 0% or greater 
interest in his business, and the business owns the property, the Board member also has an interest in the property. 
3 While an official also has an interest in his or her personal finances, including those of an immediate family 
member (Section 87103), an effect on an official's personal finances is not considered separately where the decision 
affects the official's interest in a business entity or real property. (Regulation 18702.5(c)). 


File No. A-18-055 
PageNo.4 
decision on an official's interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic 
possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701(b).) 
Additionally, Regulation 18702.2(a)(3) provides that the reasonably foreseeable financial 
effect of a governmental decision on real property is material whenever the governmental decision 
''would impose, repeal, or modify any taxes, fees, or assessments that apply to the parcel." 
Accordingly, the financial effect of the VCEA Board's decisions to establish or modify 
NEM rates will have a reasonably foreseeable and material effect on the three Board Members with 
solar panels attached to their residential or commercial properties. Absent an applicable exception, 
the financial interests would establish a potentially disqualifying conflict of interest in the VCEA 
Board's decisions regarding NEM rates. 
P11blic Ge11erally 
Section 87103 prohibits an official from taking part in a decision only if the effect of the 
decision of the official's interest is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. 
Regulation 18703(a) establishes the general rule for determining when the decision's effect on the 
official's interest is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. 
"A governmental decision's financial effect on a public official's financial 
interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the official 
establishes that a significant segment of the public is affected and the effect on his or 
her financial interest is not unique compared to the effect on the significant 
segment." 
Additionally, Regulation 18703(e) provides limited exceptions, which apply under special 
circumstances. Relevant to this request for advice, Regulation 18703(e)(l) provides a specific rule 
in the following special circumstance: 
"The decision establishes or adjusts assessments, taxes, fees, or rates for 
water, utility, or other broadly provided public services or facilities that are applied 
equally, proportionally, or by the same percentage to the official's interest and other 
businesses, properties, or individuals subject to the assessment, tax, fee, or rate." 
In this case, the VCEA Board's decisions to set NEM rates to determine how to compensate 
those customers with surplus energy generation from renewable energy systems are decisions to 
establish rates for a utility. So long as all properties subject to the NEM rates within VCEA's 
service territory will receive compensation for surplus energy generation equally, proportionally, or 
by the same percentage, a Board member with a property that uses renewable energy systems 
within VCEA 's service territory may take part in decisions to establish VCEA's NEM rates 
applicable to their properties under Regulation 18703( e)(l ). 
Accordingly, the VCEA Board members with conflicts of interests from the renewable 
energy systems attached to their residential or commercial properties may take part in VCEA Board 
decisions to set NEM rates so long as the rates are applied equally, proportionally, or by the same 
percentage to all properties with renewable energy systems within VCEA's service territory. 


SKP:jgl 
File No. A-18-055 
Page No. 5 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
By:
